reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_194145_bf16_gemm_v1_f42c9310 did recover the x32 residency damage, but it only landed at 24.881616 ms instead of the accepted 24.682431 ms anchor. The reason is visible in source: the fixed hot-band dispatch is back on the compact PTX microkernel, yet the loop body still carries the round-15 skip-final-sync seam, where `cp_async_wait_group_0()` is no longer followed by an unconditional `__syncthreads()` on the last refill boundary. That exactly explains why the branch snapped back to the 24.88 ms neighborhood rather than the clean 24.68 ms anchor. The top move is therefore not a new search family; it is to restore the full clean compact anchor by reinstating the unconditional wait/sync handoff in the PTX microkernel loop. I keep compact sync-family follow-ups second only after that clean restore is remeasured, because those edits still target the right hotspot neighborhood without reopening large-footprint families. I leave any further x32 work last, because the live evidence already says the staged family should stay closed unless its resident footprint changes materially.
family_audit: accept: the true accepted compact anchor is still the 24.682431 ms round-14 surface with the unconditional wait-group-0 sync in the PTX microkernel loop.
correct: the round-17 restore only repaired the dispatch selection; it did not restore the compact loop body, which still matches the round-15 skip-final-sync seam.
defer: compact sync-family exploration can resume only after the clean anchor is re-established and remeasured.

directions.name: Restore The Clean Compact PTX Wait-Sync Anchor After The Partial X32 Recovery
directions.hypothesis: The current run is not on the true accepted anchor yet. Reinstating the unconditional `__syncthreads()` after `cp_async_wait_group_0()` in the compact PTX loop should restore the clean round-14 anchor behavior and recover the missing ~0.20 ms without reopening any larger-footprint family.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The immediate issue is a residual sync-seam regression inside the compact PTX loop, not a broader occupancy or staging family choice.
directions.risk: Low. This is a one-site restore back to an already measured anchor behavior.
ranking_notes: This is top-ranked because the source and runtime together identify a specific residual regression that blocks the branch from reaching the accepted anchor.
It has better information value than opening a new family because the expected outcome is directly tied to a known-good prior measurement.
Frontier should prefer this over the older generic restore candidates because it encodes the exact remaining gap in the current branch state.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the restore would require edits outside the compact PTX loop handoff or if the source already contains the unconditional sync.
expected_local_changes: Reinsert the unconditional `__syncthreads()` immediately after `cp_async_wait_group_0()` in the compact PTX microkernel loop.
Leave the dispatch selection, grouped_rows=4 mapping, peeled row band, and tail path unchanged.

directions.name: Resume Compact Sync-Family Tuning Only After The Clean Anchor Returns
directions.hypothesis: Once the unconditional sync is restored and remeasured, the remaining headroom is still most likely in a carefully bounded compact sync-family tweak that does not increase register or shared-memory footprint.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual barrier overhead on the true compact PTX anchor after the seam restore.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family targets the right hotspot, but recent tiny seam experiments have mostly flattened or regressed.
ranking_notes: This family remains the best low-footprint follow-up, but it should not outrank the one-site anchor repair.
Keeping it second preserves search continuity without conflating anchor recovery with a fresh experiment.
Any revisit should explicitly avoid replaying the already measured refill-before-wait and final-no-refill-sync variants.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the proposal would duplicate the round-13 or round-15 compact seam variants.
expected_local_changes: Keep any follow-up confined to the compact PTX loop synchronization cadence after the clean anchor is re-established.

directions.name: Keep The Existing X32 Staged Family Closed Until Its Footprint Drops Materially
directions.hypothesis: The existing x32 staged family should remain deprioritized because the live data already showed a decisive occupancy and synchronization loss. It only becomes viable again if a future variant reduces its resident footprint materially.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy and latency hiding would still dominate any reopen of the current staged family.
directions.risk: High. The current staged family is already proven bad on this branch.
ranking_notes: This stays listed only to document why the staged family is not being chosen again immediately.
Any future reopen must be tied to a concrete plan to lower both registers and shared memory, not another like-for-like probe.
It ranks last because the branch still has an unfinished clean-anchor restore ahead of any justified staged-family revisit.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the proposed reopen keeps the same resident footprint class as the failed existing x32 kernel.
expected_local_changes: Do not reopen the existing x32 staged family without a concrete register/shared-memory budget reduction.

notes: Round 18/20 diagnosis emitted from the partial x32 recovery run; frontier should restore the true compact PTX wait-sync anchor before any further exploration.
